summary: Dan Shapiro addresses the conflict of interest inherent in product reviews by woodworking writers who receive free tools from vendors. Rather than pretending the conflict doesn't exist, he devises a transparent policy: accept free tools, give one away to readers, write honest reviews but simply skip reviewing tools that are bad. He frames this as a mutually beneficial 'sellout' arrangement where both writer and readers benefit from free equipment while maintaining a baseline of honesty.
key_points: Woodworking writers do reviews primarily because they get to keep the free products, creating an inherent conflict of interest
Shapiro's policy: accept free tools but give one away to readers via giveaway, only covering shipping costs
He won't write negative reviews of free tools — he'll simply not publish the review at all
For tools he paid for himself, he'll review without restraint, positive or negative
The vendor gets no editorial control over the review content
money_quotes: You may think that's because there's a high demand among readers; that our discerning eye can reveal the finer points of differentiation between identical-seeming products. Well, you'd be wrong. That's not why woodworking writers get to write reviews.
It's because they get to keep the free stuff when they're done.
So I was all ready to proceed with my career as a sellout woodworking journalist loaded with free stuff, when I hit upon a brilliant fourth option: sell out, but make you sell out with me.
If I say it's great, it's great. I won't even leave out the bad parts. I just won't write reviews of crappy tools.
If I paid for it, the gloves are off.
key_insight: Transparency about conflicts of interest — and sharing the spoils with your audience — is a more honest approach than pretending reviewer objectivity exists.
